old_claim.note: Codified Law provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: The corrected graph uses medieval notarial contracts as the specific legal-document capability; an additional edge to ancient written law codes is too broad to express that mechanism.

source_shape.source_locator: Section 2 identifies a notarial policy in 1343 and Genoese insurance regulation in 1369, providing specific medieval legal institutions rather than tracing policies directly to ancient law codes.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The historical account supplies a local notarial and regulatory setting for early policies, not a direct causal edge from ancient codification.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Replacing the category-level law claim with notarial_records keeps the legal mechanism precise and avoids counting remote legal ancestry as a direct technology dependency.

ontology_before: Maritime Insurance depended directly on Ancient Written Law Codes as a broad legal foundation.
ontology_after: Maritime Insurance uses the source-attested medieval notarial-record layer without a redundant direct edge to ancient codification.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: maritime_insurance no longer directly depends on codified_law; notarial_records now carries the documented legal mechanism.
would_reject_if: A direct historical source shows that the first Genoese premium policies specifically required the ancient code tradition represented by codified_law beyond the retained medieval notarial mechanism.
short_reason: Medieval notarial records are the specific documented legal capability; ancient law codes are too remote and broad.
